title: Mapping exposure of urban and rural communities to natural hazards
markdown:
By **Nicole Paul**, [_Global Earthquake Model Foundation_](https://www.globalquakemodel.org/), [@nicopurr] (https://twitter.com/nicopurr), 2023

**_Topic:**_ Climate, Urbanisation, Disaster risk 

**_Keywords:**_ Africa; Exposure; Disaster risk; Earthquakes; Floods; Cyclones; Buildings

**_Africapolis data_**: GIS, agglomeration data

_**Link to the original article:**_ [Development of a uniform exposure model for the African continent for use in disaster risk assessment] (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijdrr.2022.102823)

___

######*Map 1. Estimated population (left) and number of dwellings (right) in Africa, aggregated on a uniform hexagonal grid of 0.5 decimal degrees.*
![img](/images/tempimages/Paul_0_EN.png)

###Natural hazards and disaster risk in Africa 

Throughout history, Africa has been **affected by various natural hazards** including earthquakes, landslides, floods, and storms. Disasters due to natural hazards have affected approximately **half a billion people** on the continent and resulted in significant displacement, casualties, and economic loss in the past 50 years, as illustrated in the figure below. While natural hazards cannot always be avoided, the **risk that such events pose can be reduced**. For example, **early warning systems** can allow time for life-saving evacuations in advance of a tropical cyclone and **enforcement of design regulations** can prevent buildings from collapsing in an earthquake.  

######*Figure 1. Contribution of different geophysical and weather-related disaster types to reported risk metrics (total affected, number homeless, injuries, economic damage, and deaths) in Africa between 1970 and 2021 per [EM-DAT](https://www.emdat.be/).*  
![img](/images/tempimages/PAUL_1_EN.png)

To reduce disaster risk, it is critical to understand the exposure. **Exposure refers to people and assets** (e.g., buildings, infrastructure, crops) **that may be at risk in the event of a natural hazard**. Risk, such as the risk of injury or displacement, stems from the combination of exposure with hazard and vulnerability. After all, if a hazard such as a flood affects an area with no people, then no one is at risk of injury or displacement. Similarly, if a building has been designed to resist the level of wind loading from a given storm, the building is significantly less likely to become damaged and uninhabitable by its occupants.  

With the aid of national statistics and open datasets including [urban agglomeration data from Africapolis] (https://africapolis.org/en/data), the Global Earthquake Model (GEM) Foundation **developed and released a new exposure model for Africa that can be used in disaster risk assessment**. This exposure model is openly available and can be improved upon based on feedback from additional local experts or once new datasets become available. Summary information is available on a [Github repository](https://github.com/nicolepaul/africa-exposure-model) and the full datasets are available for download at the [GEM Foundations’ website](https://www.globalquakemodel.org/products?product=AfricaExposure). Details regarding the model development are published in the [International Journal for Disaster Risk Reduction](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijdrr.2022.102823). 

###Considering the variation of construction in urban settlements 

In addition to the location of people and buildings, information such as the **common construction materials** and the **configuration of the built environment** is essential to **map vulnerability to different hazards**. For example, lightweight construction such as dwellings made using straw/vegetative materials tend to be more vulnerable to cyclones. In contrast, dwellings constructed using adobe/mud bricks tend to be particularly vulnerable to earthquakes. However, the typical building materials used for building construction **differs widely between urban and rural settlements**. Reinforced concrete structures are more prevalent in urban centres, while dwellings constructed of locally sourced materials such as straw and mud are more common in rural areas. This discrepancy in construction practices is shown in the figure below.  

######*Map 2. Modelled distribution of (left) reinforced concrete or confined masonry and (right) straw/vegetative residential buildings in Africa, aggregated on a uniform hexagonal grid of 0.5 decimal degrees. Concrete and confined masonry buildings are concentrated around urban areas, whereas straw/vegetative buildings are more prevalent in rural areas.* 
![img](/images/tempimages/Paul_3_EN.png)


To this end, the data from Africapolis regarding urban agglomerations was used to **ensure that the information available from national statistics and other data sources would be distributed in a realistic manner for urban and rural areas separately.** By reflecting the distribution of population and buildings in a realistic manner, a **more accurate view of disaster risk can be estimated**, potentially highlighting hot spots of urban disaster risk. 

###Considering future exposure and risk to natural hazards 

In addition to significant overall population growth anticipated in Africa during the forthcoming decades, **the proportion of population in urban areas is expected to significantly increase**. These trends will shape the disaster risk of the continent. For example, **increased populations in hazard-prone areas could increase disaster risk**, whereas implementation and enforcement of building design regulations in newly constructed buildings can decrease disaster risk. 

######*Figure 2. Estimated and projected population for different [Sustainable Development Goal (SDG)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/sustainable-development-goals) regions between years 1950 and 2100, per the [United Nationals World Population Prospects (2019)](https://population.un.org/wpp/Publications/), where the shaded range indicates the 95%ile range.* 

![img](/images/tempimages/Paul_4_EN.png)

######*Figure 3. Estimated and projected proportion of the African population in urban areas between years 1950 and 2050, per the [United Nationals World Urbanization Prospects (2018)](https://population.un.org/wup/publications/Files/WUP2018-Report.pdf).* 
![img](/images/tempimages/Paul_5_EN.png)

To capture how disaster risk might evolve under these ongoing trends, the Africa Exposure Model released by the GEM Foundation **modelled both the current exposure (2020) and projected the exposure to future years (2025, 2030, 2035, 2040, 2045, and 2050).** According to the model, the number of total buildings is expected to **double** in many regions (i.e., Central, East, and West Africa) and the number of urban buildings is expected to nearly **triple** in some regions (i.e., Central and East Africa). With that number of buildings yet-to-be-built over the next few years, there is a massive opportunity to **reduce future disaster risk through risk-informed design and planning in Africa.**

With an open exposure model covering the African continent now available, further studies can assess disaster risk and prioritize and evaluate potential disaster mitigations. The same authors of this exposure model are currently investigating the potential impact of earthquakes in this region. An article summarizing an earthquake risk model for Africa is expected to be published in 2023. 

*The views, conclusions and materials of external contributors do not represent the views of OECD/SWAC or its Members.*

####*Author’s biography* 

![img](/images/tempimages/Paul_6.jpg)

*[Nicole Paul](https://twitter.com/nicopurr) is a disaster risk analyst based in Milan, Italy. She currently works at the Global Earthquake Model Foundation and studies at the Institute for Risk and Disaster Reduction at University College London.*
